cards.hook: Forget AAA vs indie. A fun game is a fun game.
cards.caption: Forget AAA vs indie labels. There are more releases now than you'll ever finish — an enjoyable game is enjoyable, full stop. The takes my feed keeps over the timeline noise.
cards.stance: The AAA/AA/indie distinction is marketing, not quality.

cards.hook: The Dreamcast still gets new physical indie releases in 2026.
cards.caption: The Dreamcast still gets fresh physical indie releases decades later — homebrew scenes outlive the corporations that killed them. Niche gem my feed surfaced.

cards.hook: Sells good? Studio gone. Sells bad? Studio gone.
cards.caption: Sells good — studio gone. Sells bad — studio gone. The games industry needs a hard reset and everyone's finally saying it out loud. The take my feed caught before it blew up.
cards.stance: No sales number saves a studio anymore — the model itself is broken.

cards.hook: 96% of indie games make no money.
cards.caption: 96% of indie games make almost nothing — most devs never see a payout. The number nobody wants on the store page. The stats my feed keeps before the hype buries them.
cards.stance: Indie dev is a lottery dressed up as a career path.

cards.hook: PlayStation ditching physical was its worst call yet.
cards.caption: A dev who shipped a physical indie says the market never left — PlayStation walked away from real demand. The industry take my feed flagged before the timeline did.
cards.stance: Killing physical media is corporate convenience, not consumer demand.

cards.hook: Should you actually make a video game?
cards.caption: Real devs behind Quartet and Geo Mythica break down what shipping an indie game actually costs you. The builder talk my feed keeps over the hype.

cards.hook: 1 year of game dev, zero prior experience — use Godot.
cards.caption: One year of game dev from zero, no experience — and the verdict is just use Godot. The open-source path most tutorials skip. The builder finds my feed keeps.
cards.stance: You don't need Unity or Unreal to start — Godot is enough.

cards.hook: No game over sixty quid is more than a 7/10
cards.caption: No game over sixty quid is more than a 7/10. A blunt rule for scoring games by price, not hype. The kind of sharp take my feed keeps and the timeline buries.
cards.stance: Price should cap the score: charge AAA money, you don't get to hide behind indie grading.

cards.hook: Indie devs can't do $100 deluxe editions. Build a great game instead.
cards.caption: Indie devs can't fund $100 deluxe editions, so stop expecting them to. Make a kick-ass product and players come. The dev-scene talk my feed surfaces before the timeline.
cards.stance: Deluxe editions are a big-publisher tax; a great small game needs none of it.

cards.hook: 14 people, most part-time, run this game studio
triggers: weak: reply fragment, no standalone context

cards.hook: Steam and GoG made gamers stop caring about physical copies
cards.caption: Steam and GoG got so trustworthy that all-digital libraries stopped scaring anyone. Most PC devs skip physical releases now. The shift my feed catches before the timeline argues about it.
cards.stance: Physical PC releases are nostalgia, not preservation; trust killed the disc.

cards.hook: A fresh batch of indie games dropped on Steam and Switch today
cards.caption: A fresh batch of smaller indie titles just launched on Steam and Switch. The releases worth playing hide under the AAA noise. This is the stuff my feed keeps for you.

cards.hook: Dev shows off new scoreboard animations, live on itch
triggers: weak: build-log fragment, no hook payoff

cards.hook: An atmospheric driving game through Dutch dikes and windmills
cards.caption: Lowlands Racer: an atmospheric driving game through Dutch polders, dikes and windmills, built in public. The oddly specific indie gems my feed finds before the timeline.

cards.hook: 99k views, 10 years of free games, funded only by donations
cards.caption: 99k views. 10+ years of gamedev. Every game free, funded purely by voluntary donations. Proof you can build without a paywall. The wins my feed keeps that the timeline never shows you.
cards.stance: Free-plus-donations is a real business model, not charity.

cards.hook: Watch a pixel-art dungeon get its skill tree
cards.caption: Watch a pixel-art dungeon grow its skill tree in real time. Solo devs building in the open are the best part of gaming. This gem surfaced before the timeline caught it.
cards.stance: The best new releases are being built in public by one person right now.

cards.hook: Reusing game assets is good, actually
cards.caption: Hot take: devs reusing assets isn't lazy — it's how good games ship on time. The purists are wrong. This is the kind of debate my feed keeps and the noise it drops.
cards.stance: Asset reuse ships games. Purists who call it lazy have never shipped anything.

cards.hook: We traded brilliant enemy AI for pretty graphics
cards.caption: Modern game enemies are brain-dead — and prettier graphics are why. You noticed it too. This is the sharp take my feed keeps instead of the review-score noise.
cards.stance: Enemy AI peaked years ago. We spent the whole budget on lighting instead.

cards.hook: New indie release drops July 2026
cards.caption: A niche indie game announcement with low signal for a broad tech audience — reads as a single project's promo, not a curated gem.

cards.hook: Your first favorite game may be gone forever
cards.caption: Your first favorite mobile game might be unplayable now — pulled, delisted, gone. Game preservation is a real crisis. The stuff my feed surfaces that the timeline forgets.
cards.stance: Delisting games is quietly erasing a whole generation's gaming history.

cards.hook: What genre even means in game design
cards.caption: A Patreon-gated design-theory video — thin hook, mostly a channel plug. Low standalone signal for the feed.

cards.hook: What actually happens when your game ships?
cards.caption: What really happens the moment an indie game releases — is it all bugfixing hell, or more? A genuine question worth answering. The kind of thread my feed keeps.

cards.hook: This week's Nintendo eShop new releases
cards.caption: A generic weekly eShop roundup blurb — platform-marketing noise, no angle or curation value for a tech audience.

cards.hook: Want to ship a game? Start here, not with an engine.
cards.caption: Want to ship a game? Start here, not with a $0 engine download. A beginner indie dev guide that skips the fluff. The gamedev stuff my feed keeps before the tutorials pile up.
cards.stance: Most "learn gamedev" advice starts with the wrong tool.

cards.hook: The indie devs building games that actually matter.
cards.caption: The indie devs building games that actually matter, not just clones. A talk on why small studios keep out-caring the big ones. Found in my feed before the timeline surfaced it.

cards.hook: Agents of Groove just teased a full soundtrack overhaul.
cards.caption: Agents of Groove just teased a full soundtrack and art overhaul. This is the indie release you want to watch before it blows up. The stuff my feed keeps before the hype hits.

cards.hook: Big AI labs chase trillion-dollar infra. Indies already ship with it.
cards.caption: Big AI labs chase trillion-dollar infra. Meanwhile indie studios use AI to punch way above their weight, today. The contrarian take my feed kept before the timeline caught up.
cards.stance: The real AI game revolution is happening in tiny studios, not frontier labs.

cards.hook: A whole game about sledding just dropped on Game Pass.
cards.caption: A whole game about sledding just dropped on Game Pass, and it looks absurdly fun. From a dev who clearly loves it. The kind of hidden release my feed keeps before you scroll past it.

cards.hook: In Fresco, the Egyptian carvings ARE the playable level.
cards.caption: In this upcoming indie game Fresco, the Egyptian wall carvings are the actual playable environment. Wild idea, gorgeous execution. Found before the timeline in my feed.
cards.stance: The best art direction this year is coming from a solo indie, not a AAA studio.

cards.hook: The part of your game that breaks with zero code changes.
cards.caption: That feeling when a feature you haven't touched in months just silently breaks. Every gamedev knows the dread. The relatable stuff my feed keeps.

cards.hook: You don't need a Hollywood budget. You need to split the tasks.
cards.caption: You don't need a Hollywood budget for game art, you need to know which tasks to hand AI and which to do by hand. A brutal-honest hybrid pipeline. The AI stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: AI won't replace your art team, but knowing what to hand it will replace your budget.
